coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of core–shell Ag@CDs nanoprobes from sweet lemon waste for selective fluorescence detection of losartan in biofluids
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Safarpoor, Mohammad
Ghaedi, Mehrorang
Dinarvand, Rassoul
Asfaram, Arash
coredata.dc:description: Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, such as losartan (LST), are widely used in the treatment of hypertension, making their rapid and reliable detection highly desirable. This study, therefore, presents a selective and cost-effective fluorescence sensor for the detection of LST using core–shell AgNPs@CDs nanoparticles (NPs) as a nanoprobe. Firstly, the nanoprobes were synthesized using carbon dots (CDs) derived from Sweet lemon waste through an environmentally friendly process, where the CDs directly reduced Ag + to AgNPs. Subsequently, key sensing parameters, including nanoprobe volume and incubation time, were optimized. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobes at 420 nm exhibited a significant blue shift and increased proportionally with the LST concentration, ranging from 0.5 μM to 45 μM. Moreover, the response was linear over the range of 0.5 to 4.5 μM, with a detection limit (LOD) of 180 nM. This study demonstrates a green and economical strategy for LST detection in biofluids, utilizing waste-derived nanomaterials for fluorescence-based sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Losartan
Core-shell Nanoprobes
Green Synthesis
Citrus limetta
Fluorescence Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for colorimetric detection of LST
Principle of detection of LST using the nanoprobe

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate the financial support from Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran, to conduct this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a, b) and Histogram (c) of the synthesized AgNPs@CDs.
EDX analysis (a) and elemental mapping (b) of the synthesized AgNPs@CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of CDs, AgNO3 and AgNPs@CDs, (b) UV–Vis and (c) Emission spectra of CDs and synthesized AgNPs@CDs.
PL spectra of the different solutions: CDs, AgNPs@CDs, LST (40 µM), a mixture of AgNPs@CDs in the presence of LST.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of AgNPs@CDs aqueous solution under the different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 380 nm. (b) Effect of AgNPs@CDs volume from 50 μL to 400 μL in containing and LST (40 µM), (c) Effect of incubation time from 0 min to 9 min for 200 μL AgNPs@CDs containing LST (40 µM).

(A) The recorded absorbance spectra and (B) linear calibration curve for different concentrations of LST ranging from 0.5 μM to 45 μM in the presence of the nanoprobes under the optimum condition. (C) The response of the developed sensor towards LST compared to some ions and drugs including VAL, AML (30 μM) and also Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cl−, Br−, NO3 − and HCO3 with 100-fold concentration than LST (30 μM).
A schematical presentation for (A) the synthesis steps of core–shell AgNPs@CDs and (B) possible mechanism for detection of LST by the core–shell AgNPs@CDs.
A comparison of the applicability of the proposed sensor with other reported sensors for LST detection.
Detection of HEP in human urine, serum samples and injection sample using the proposed sensor.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of core–shell Ag@CDs nanoprobes from sweet lemon waste for selective fluorescence detection of losartan in biofluids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Yasouj University, Yasouj 75918-74831, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Yasouj University, Yasouj 75918-74831, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medicinal Plants Research Center, Yasuj University of Medical Sciences, Yasuj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medicinal Plants Research Center, Yasuj University of Medical Sciences, Yasuj, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, such as losartan (LST), are widely used in the treatment of hypertension, making their rapid and reliable detection highly desirable. This study, therefore, presents a selective and cost-effective fluorescence sensor for the detection of LST using core–shell AgNPs@CDs nanoparticles (NPs) as a nanoprobe. Firstly, the nanoprobes were synthesized using carbon dots (CDs) derived from Sweet lemon waste through an environmentally friendly process, where the CDs directly reduced Ag + to AgNPs. Subsequently, key sensing parameters, including nanoprobe volume and incubation time, were optimized. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobes at 420 nm exhibited a significant blue shift and increased proportionally with the LST concentration, ranging from 0.5 μM to 45 μM. Moreover, the response was linear over the range of 0.5 to 4.5 μM, with a detection limit (LOD) of 180 nM. This study demonstrates a green and economical strategy for LST detection in biofluids, utilizing waste-derived nanomaterials for fluorescence-based sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical drugs play a critical role in treating, curing, and preventing diseases, and one such essential drug is losartan (LST), an angiotensin II receptor antagonist, an angiotensin II receptor antagonist listed by the World Health Organization (WHO) as an essential medicine [1,2]. Losartan is commonly prescribed for the treatment of high blood pressure, reducing the risk of stroke, heart failure, and kidney complications, especially in patients with type 2 diabetes​. As a highly effective non-peptide angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), losartan works by relaxing blood vessels and lowering blood pressure, thus improving blood flow and oxygen supply to the heart​ [3,4]. Several analytical techniques have been developed for the detection of LST, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5–8], LC-MS [9,10], voltammetry [11], complex formation [12], charge-transfer complexes [13], spectrophotometry [14] and spectrofluorimetry [3,15–19]. However, many of these methods are costly, technically demanding, and rely on specialized instrumentation and hazardous solvents. Fluorescence spectroscopy, on the other hand, offers several advantages, such as simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and high sensitivity, making it a favorable alternative [2,20–22].

Carbon nanostructures, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, carbon nanofibers (CNFs), fullerenes, nanodiamonds (NDs), carbon dots (CDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have garnered significant research attention due to their exceptional physicochemical and optoelectronic properties, often surpassing those of metal nanostructures, semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs), organic dyes, and conjugated polymers [23–25]. Among them, CDs stand out as the smallest and brightest, enabling applications in optical sensors, biosensors, bioimaging, fluorescent inks, electronic devices, and drug delivery, thanks to their photoreactivity, chemical stability, cost-efficiency, biocompatibility, and tunable luminescence [26].. To synthesize CDs, numerous “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches have been explored [26], including arc discharge [27], laser ablation [28], microwave pyrolysis [29], and hydrothermal oxidation [30]. However, these techniques often involve expensive, complex, multi-step procedures requiring acidic media and extensive post-synthesis modifications to achieve high quantum yields (QY). Techniques such as heteroatom doping or surface passivation are commonly used to enhance QY but add to the challenges of scalability and cost-effectiveness [31]. In contrast, green synthesis has emerged as a sustainable and environmentally friendly alternative, leveraging natural sources like carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids. This approach eliminates the use of toxic chemicals and aligns with the growing demand for eco-friendly materials. Examples include synthesizing carbon dots (GCDs) from fruit peels [32–34], whey [35], coffee grounds [36], agricultural residues [37], waste oils [38], and paper [39]. Such methods utilize abundant and inexpensive precursors, offering the dual advantage of waste valorization and the production of high-quality CDs. which largely depends on the choice of precursors and synthesis processes. Developing innovative synthetic routes and exploring novel precursor materials are crucial steps toward producing commercially viable CDs with broad applications [40]. Citrus limetta, or sweet lemon, is a promising precursor due to its rich content of citric acid, vitamins, and sugars. Its pulp can serve as both a carbon source and a passivation agent during synthesis. Moreover, composites of CDs with metal nanoparticles (MNPs@CDs) combine the optical benefits of both materials, with CDs directly reducing and stabilizing metal-ion solutions, further expanding their utility in various fields.

In this study, a green synthesis of CDs from sweet lemon (Citrus limetta) waste is reported, along with their application in reducing Ag+ ions to Ag0, forming core–shell AgNPs@CDs nanoprobes. These nanoprobes were used for the rapid detection of LST in biofluids, demonstrating excellent sensitivity, selectivity, and cost-efficiency. This approach not only provides a practical solution for LST detection but also contributes to sustainable nanomaterial development through food waste utilization.

Losartan (LST), valsartan (VAL), and amlodipine (AML) were purchased from Pharmed Pars and Raha Pharmaceutical Companies in Iran. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) and other analytical-grade reagents were obtained from Merck (Germany) and Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Milli-Q deionized water (DI) water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ was used for all tests. Citrus limetta fruits were sourced from a local market in Tehran. LST tablets (25 mg) were purchased from a local market in Tehran, and LST tablets (25 mg) were purchased from a local pharmacy. Human biofluids, including urine and serum samples, were collected from healthy volunteers at Imam Khomeini Hospital, Tehran.

FT-IR spectra were recorded using a Jasco 460 FT-IR spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with KBr pellet technique. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the synthesized AgNPs@CDs were obtained using a Philips EM 208 microscope operated at 100 kV. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and elemental mapping analyses were performed using an FE-SEM TESCAN MIRA3 instrument equipped with an EDX detector. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using an FS-5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK), and UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Jasco 730 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Japan).

The core–shell AgNPs@CDs were synthesized in two steps. First, carbon dots (CDs) were produced from Citrus limetta pulp waste through pyrolysis at 190 °C for 20 min in a hot air oven. The resulting yellowish residue was dissolved in DI water and filtered using a 0.2 µm syringe filter to remove unreacted precursors. In the second step, AgNO3 (1 mL, 0.01 mol L−1) and CDs (0.05 g in 30 mL) were mixed in a 1:6 ratio with 0.5 mL of 0.06 mol L−1 ammonia solution. The mixture was stirred for 20 min at room temperature, during which the solution color changed from light yellow to orange, indicating the formation of core–shell AgNPs@CDs [41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for colorimetric detection of LST
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensor's performance in detecting LST, varying concentrations of LST were added to 200 μL of synthesized AgNPs@CDs nanoprobes. The final volume of the solution was adjusted to 2 mL with DI water, and after 6 min of incubation, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded under excitation at 340 nm.

Urine and serum samples were collected from healthy individuals. Serum and urine samples were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min. A 100 μL aliquot of each sample was diluted with DI water in a 10 mL volumetric flask. To these samples, known concentrations of LST were added, yielding final concentrations of 5, 15, and 30 μM. The developed sensor was used to analyze these samples, and each measurement was performed in triplicate.

To assess the selectivity of the sensor, common interfering ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, HCO3 −) and substrates (VAL, AML) were tested at a concentration of 30 μM. Each interferent was mixed with 200 μL of AgNPs@CDs, and the final volume was adjusted to 2 mL with DI water. Fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 340 nm.

In this study, CDs derived from sweet lemon waste were employed as both reducing and stabilizing agents for the synthesis of core–shell AgNPs@CDs. TEM analysis (Fig. 1 a and b) and and color contrast between the shell and core confirmed the formation of core–shell nanostructures, with an average nanoparticle diameter of approximately 14 ± 2.1 nm (Fig. 1c) [43]. EDX analysis and elemental mapping (Fig. 2 ) verified the presence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and silver (Ag), confirming successful nanoprobe synthesis. To analyze the synthesized product and compare its functional groups to the starting materials, FTIR spectra of CDs, AgNO3, and AgNPs@CDs were recorded. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3 a), a broad peak at 3300 cm−1 corresponds to –OH stretching vibrations, while peaks at 2930 cm−1, 2090 cm−1, and 1620 cm−1 are attributed to C-H, C≡C, and C=C stretching, respectively. A peak at 1734 cm−1 indicates C=O groups from carboxylic acids or esters, and peaks at 1420 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 correspond to –CH3 bending and C-O stretching, respectively [44]. The FTIR spectrum of AgNO3 shows peaks at 3470, 2391, 1758, 1500, and 790 cm−1, indicating Ag+ ions and N-O bonds [45]. During AgNP synthesis, shifts in absorption peaks (e.g., 3470 to 3300 cm−1 for –NH, 2391 to 2090 cm−1, 1500 to 1643 cm−1 for C=O, and 790 to 620 cm−1) confirm interactions between CDs and AgNO3. Increased intensity of –OH and C=O peaks and the disappearance of the 1050 cm−1 peak further validate the successful synthesis of AgNPs@CDs [46]. The UV–Vis spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3b) shows a sharp peak at 290 nm, corresponding to n → π* and π → π* transitions of C=O and C=C groups [26,41]. For AgNPs@CDs, the same 290 nm peak appears alongside a new peak at 450 nm, characteristic of AgNP surface plasmon resonance. These peaks confirm the AgNP core is encapsulated by CDs, with CDs acting as reducing and stabilizing agents. The color change from yellow (CDs) to orange further indicates successful AgNPs@CDs synthesis. The fluorescence spectrum of CDs presented a peak at 420 nm (Fig. 3c), which decreased in intensity upon the formation of AgNPs@CDs, further confirming the successful synthesis of the nanoprobes [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of detection of LST using the nanoprobe

ce:section.ce:para: To ensure optimal sensor performance, several experimental parameters were investigated, including excitation wavelength, nanoprobe volume, and incubation time. As shown in Fig. 5 a, increasing the excitation wavelength (λex) from 300 nm to 340 nm resulted in enhanced fluorescence intensity, with 340 nm providing the maximum response. The effect of nanoprobe volume on sensor response was studied by varying the volume from 50 μL to 400 μL (Fig. 5b). A volume of 200 μL provided the highest and most stable signal and was selected for further experiments. Additionally, the reaction time was optimized, with the highest fluorescence intensity observed after 6 min of incubation (Fig. 5c).

To assess the selectivity of the AgNPs@CDs sensor, potential interfering ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, HCO3 −) and drugs (VAL, AML) were tested at concentrations higher than that of LST. As shown in Fig. 6c, the presence of these ions and drugs did not significantly affect the sensor's fluorescence signal, demonstrating high selectivity toward LST.

The sensor's applicability was further tested by analyzing LST in human serum, urine, and pharmaceutical tablet samples. As summarized in Table 2 , the concentrations of LST in the real samples were consistent with the expected values. The calculated recovery rates (95.40 % to 104.80 %) and RSD values (less than 3 %) confirmed the sensor's accuracy and precision, indicating its suitability for real sample analysis.

In this study, we developed a green and cost-effective fluorescence-based sensor for detecting losartan (LST) using core–shell AgNPs@CDs nanoprobes derived from sweet lemon waste. The nanoprobes were synthesized through an environmentally friendly method, and their morphological and optical properties were characterized. The sensor showed a wide detection range, high sensitivity, and excellent selectivity for LST, even in the presence of potential interferents. Additionally, it demonstrated high accuracy and precision in detecting LST in real human biofluids and pharmaceutical samples. This work offers a sustainable and efficient approach for LST detection, with potential applications in clinical diagnostics and environmental monitoring. Future studies could extend this method to other pharmaceutical compounds or adapt it for use in portable diagnostic devices.

Mohammad Safarpoor: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mehrorang Ghaedi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Rassoul Dinarvand: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Arash Asfaram: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation.
This study was supported by a fund provided by the Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the financial support from Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran, to conduct this study.
